narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication with your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.80, which stands out well above the typical student's average of 3.21, and your middle family income background reflects the resilience many from similar circumstances bring to their pursuits. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%, and your Year 1 school decile of 5 is close to average, while your Year 3 decile of 4 sits a bit below. However, our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 55.6% probability that you would fail it—tipping the scales just enough to lead to the regrettable rejection of your application. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 27, which is notably lower than the average of 36.7 and had the strongest pull in that direction. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your excellent undergraduate GPA, which significantly nudged the prediction toward expecting you to pass, acting as the top positive influence in your favor. Your Year 3 decile ranking then played a secondary role in increasing the failure risk, while your family income had a smaller but similar push. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile and full-time status offered minor boosts toward success. Compared to typical students, your GPA shines brightly as a strength many lack, but the LSAT gap is what set you apart in a challenging way for this model. We know this outcome can feel disheartening, especially given the unique barriers Black women often face in competitive fields like law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it carries the most weight here—many students improve dramatically with targeted prep. Strengthening your later academic deciles through focused effort could also help, and exploring programs with holistic reviews or bar passage support tailored to diverse backgrounds might open new doors. You're clearly capable, and with these steps, a stronger prediction awaits next time. Keep pushing—you've got this.
